title: Texturing + Materials
objective: Dress your character in its final colors and materials—metal should catch light, skin should read soft, cloth should stay matte. AI lays down the base coat, you hand-paint the story, and together you build a complete set of PBR maps. This is where the character earns its visual identity.

sections.h2: From gray model to flesh and blood
paras: Picking up from Modules 05 + 06: you've got clean UVs and baked normal/AO/curvature/ID maps—now turn them into real materials.

Texturing isn't just "painting colors on." Modern games use PBR (physically based rendering): you define what material each surface is, and the engine lights it by the rules of physics. So what you're painting isn't one image—it's a set of maps, each describing color, whether it's metal, how rough it is, and where it glows.

sections.h2: Five maps that define every material
paras: Understand these five (the metallic/roughness workflow) and materials stop being a mystery:
Base Color — the raw surface color, no lighting baked in! Leave light to the engine
Metallic — is it metal or not, basically black or white
Roughness — rough (white) or smooth (black), the channel that tells the most story
Normal — from Module 06, surface bumps and detail
Emissive — the parts that glow on their own: eyes, runes, screens
box: Two make-or-break rules: ① Base Color must be "flat"—never paint shadows or highlights into it, or the moment the engine lights it, everything reads dirty; ② Roughness is the soul—wear, grime, new vs. old, it all lives here. Whether a character reads believable is 70% roughness.

sections.h2: Let AI do it all, or take full hand control?

branches.name: AI does it all
branches.p: Meshy text-to-texture / image-to-texture: one prompt yields a full PBR set (base color + roughness + metallic + normal + emissive). Good enough for background characters and quick color tests.

branches.name: Substance hand control
branches.p: Substance Painter, using Module 06's bakes to drive wear, full control with layers + masks. The ceiling for hero characters.

branches.name: Hybrid
branches.p: AI lays the base, Substance refines. AI skips the tedious under-painting so you can spend your effort on the face, seams, and story details.
branches.code: 快又可控 · 本节主推

paras: In Substance, the maps you baked in 06 aren't there to look at—they're there to drive detail: procedurally generating realistic wear ten times faster than painting by hand:
Curvature → drives edge wear: raised edges automatically expose metal / chip paint.
AO → drives grime in crevices: seams and recesses automatically collect dust and dirt.
Material ID → instant selections: one click selects "all metal parts" or "all leather," then texture each separately.

sections.h2: From a single prompt to a finished texture set

substeps.title: AI base-color first pass
paras: First let Meshy texture a pass onto your own UVs (turn on enable_original_uv) to get a complete PBR starting point. The more specific the prompt, the better: material + finish + color + amount of wear + style.
For multi-part characters you can give each material slot its own prompt, or use Meshy's local re-texture (select the helmet/sword and change it alone).

prompts.label: Meshy Texture · text-to-texture
prompts.text:
[材料+颜色+做工+磨损], PBR, [stylized game / semi-realistic],
clean readable surface

示例: weathered steel armor with gold trim, muted blue cloth,
leather straps, subtle edge wear, stylized game character, PBR

calls.text: Turn on "remove highlights/shadows" to flatten the base color—this is exactly the clean albedo PBR wants, and the engine handles lighting itself. Use ChatGPT to turn a vague idea into a specific prompt like the one above for a better hit rate.

substeps.title: Into Substance + load the bakes
bullets: Import the UV'd low-poly into Substance Painter.
Load the Normal / AO / Curvature / ID / Position / Thickness baked in 06 into Mesh Maps.
The AI base color can come in as a base layer to refine on top of.

substeps.title: Lay down base materials
bullets: Zone by part with the ID mask: build a layer group each for metal, leather, cloth, and skin.
Drop a smart material / base material into each group first, setting the broad direction for base color + roughness + metallic.
This step gets the character "in the ballpark" first; detail comes later.

substeps.title: Add detail and story
bullets: Use a curvature-driven generator to add edge wear (metal edges go bright, paint chips off).
Use AO-driven grime and dust in recesses and seams.
Hand-paint the finishing touches: the face, scars, insignia, and any seams that read wrong—paint them yourself.
Tell a story: is this gear brand-new or battle-worn? Where and how much it's worn IS the narrative.

substeps.title: Calibrate roughness / metallic
bullets: Metal has to read as metal: metallic maxed out, roughness varied (fingerprints, oil film, scratches).
Skin/cloth are non-metal: metallic at 0, roughness alone separating silk (smooth) from coarse linen (matte).
Rotate the light and watch the highlights—it's the fastest way to check whether the PBR is right.

substeps.title: Emissive
paras: Put Emissive on whatever should glow: energy runes, screens, lamps. Give the eyes a gold emissive so they light up in the dark—that's the Bluecat signature 🐾.

substeps.title: Export
bullets: Pick the export preset for your engine (Unity / Unreal / glTF) so channel packing lines up automatically.
Confirm base color is flat (no lighting baked in).
Resolution: 2K–4K for a hero. Name them char_basecolor / _roughness / _metallic / _normal / _emissive and archive into 07_texture.

box: ① Keep base color flat, leave light to the engine. Painting shadows and highlights into base color is the deadliest beginner mistake—change the light and it all goes dirty.
② Roughness tells the story. The same metal reads new, oily, or worn entirely through roughness. It sets the tone more than color does.
③ Let the bakes do the work. Generate wear and grime procedurally from curvature/AO—don't paint it stroke by stroke, it's slow and fake.

sections.h2: These bite the moment the light changes
items: Painting lighting into base color → the engine lights it, fake shadows stack on real ones, dirty.
Dead-flat roughness → every material looks like the same plastic.
Smearing mid-gray metallic everywhere → reads as neither metal nor non-metal, just weird.
Hand-painting all the wear → slow and unnatural, worse than curvature/AO driving it.
Shipping the AI base color unretouched → seams and the face fall apart under a close-up.
Export preset not matching the engine → channels misalign, everything goes black or glowing in-engine.
Not enough resolution / lighting not removed → blurry up close, dirty when the light changes.

items.text: Base color is flat, no lighting
Materials read right: metal like metal, cloth like cloth
items.small: verify by rotating the light on the highlights

items.text: Wear/grime driven by bakes, face hand-retouched
items.small: has a story, holds up in close-up

items.text: Exported with the target engine's preset, named and archived

sections.title: 🐾 Three questions, pass all to proceed
questions: Rotate the light—does every material read right (metal reflective, skin soft, cloth matte)?
Is the base color flat, with no lighting baked in?
Are the maps exported with the engine preset, named and archived?
sections.pass: ▸ All three pass → on to Module 08: Rigging + Animation (🐾 hands-on, the last tough nut).
